dateStatements.label: revision and expansion
dateStatements.notes: The header was created on 21 February 2023; the revision entry records the substantive work on 13 February.

entities.description: The 2023 MEC/CME encoding made from OTA 0174 and checked against Cigman's print edition.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text CME90174
notes: The encoding has 18 DIV1 units, 811 paragraphs, 1,712 line milestones, and 350 manuscript-folio milestones: 213 for A, 128 for R, and 9 for M.
Those 18 editorial/XML units can also be described as 17 writings when optional expansion 11A is counted as part of sermon 11 rather than as an independent work.
It follows print-edition line breaks but omits the edition's italics, apparatus, printed-page references, introduction, and notes.
Square brackets remain literal; angle-bracketed source text was mapped to SUPPLIED, with the header warning that UNCLEAR may sometimes have been more appropriate.

rights.basis: CME90174's embedded AVAILABILITY statement says Oxford, and consequently U-M, distributes the text under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 Unported.

This record pins the first retained bundle; member SHA-256 values are b9e2a0e3797de40195c32dd3b5e9bd5d12adf6eef4414f040690154376030cfb (header), c817274cfebe245cd5bbe4992c0c5f9db526cf04d8aa853d865bcef04b04e555 (text), and cf4991b00b9d746473737f294564fec3186b32b74d3fb36f81e3125d834f41e5 (readme).

rights.basis: The official item record and embedded file header explicitly distribute the resource under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 Unported.
rights.component: OTA digital files and encoded transcription as distributed
notes: Noncommercial and ShareAlike conditions matter for repository reuse; the license does not automatically clear any independently protected EETS editorial material.

entities.description: The three-file legacy OTA deposit from which the 2023 U-M text began.
entities.label: Oxford Text Archive legacy digital text ota:0174
notes: The current package contains header0174.xml, lolls1to16-0174.txt, and readme0174.txt; ZIP integrity testing succeeded.

The readme literally attributes its source to an Early English Text Society edition dated 1988, whereas header0174.xml identifies the source as Cigman's 1989 publication and the edition itself was published in 1989. This conflict is preserved rather than normalized.

The text is incomplete relative to Cigman's edition: actual sermon 13 is absent, sermon 4 lacks the first five or six letters of most lines, and the Sermon of Dead Men is numbered as sermon 13 in the plain text.
The readme's statement that dead.txt was unavailable conflicts with the current bundled plain text, which contains the Sermon of Dead Men under the wrong number.

bibliographic.title: Lollard Sermons

entities.label: Gloria Cigman's Lollard Sermons (1989)
notes: The edition establishes the selection represented as 18 top-level units in CME90174; scholarship may count 17 writings when optional expansion 11A is treated as part of sermon 11. Its introduction and apparatus were not encoded by U-M.
Direct consultation of the edition's full introductory argument and apparatus remains necessary to refine the precise witness-to-reading relationships.

rights.basis: The underlying anonymous texts were created and fixed in surviving manuscripts in the late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; any copyright in that medieval expression has long expired. This conclusion does not extend to modern editorial or photographic layers.
rights.component: Anonymous medieval sermon texts as such, excluding modern transcription, selection, apparatus, and typography

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1989 and no public-domain or open-license statement for its modern scholarly content was found in the checked official catalog and publisher records.
rights.component: Modern editorial selection, transcription, introduction, apparatus, notes, and typography
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and other applicable jurisdictions
notes: This cautious status does not deny that individual facts, uncopyrightable readings, quotation exceptions, or other lawful uses may be available. It records that the modern edition as a whole is not verified open.

rights.basis: The February 2026 image-order form says images of out-of-copyright material are supplied under CC BY-NC 4.0 for noncommercial reuse. The Imaging Services terms permit specified personal, noncommercial, and academic uses while requiring separate treatment for commercial use, but the separately linked Image Order Terms and Conditions say Bodleian retains copyright in reproductions and supplied material may not be reproduced without written permission. The conflict is preserved and requires item-specific clarification.

localCopies.label: Manchester English MS 412 IIIF Presentation manifest
notes: Only the IIIF metadata manifest is cached; no manuscript page images were downloaded.
The target work is at fols 50r–72r / canvases 138–182.

The /view/138 and /view/182 alternates are working human-readable boundary-page links. The exact Image API alternates are also working boundary images for fols 50r and 72r; the canvas identifiers recorded by the manifest themselves return HTTP 404 and therefore remain locators rather than links.
Only provider metadata is cached locally. Public viewing does not itself authorize whole-object systematic image extraction or repository redistribution.

rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest combines an all-rights-reserved attribution with CC BY-NC 4.0 language for downloadable images, while the site terms reserve rights, permit limited personal noncommercial extracts, and prohibit systematic extraction or reutilization of substantial parts.

relations.subjectLabel: University of Michigan CME digital text CME90174

assertion.notes: The relationship is secure at the edition-text level but not yet at the level of a specific printed copy versus editorial typescript or machine-readable precursor.

scope.notes: OTA and U-M describe the digital text as taken from, derived from, or closely associated with the edition. The OTA readme literally calls its source an EETS edition dated 1988, while header0174.xml and the published edition give 1989. The 29 April 1989 readme date and this source-date conflict may indicate a pre-publication editorial file, so the exact physical mediation remains unresolved.

relations.subjectLabel: Oxford Text Archive legacy digital text ota:0174

scope.notes: The BL child records give incipit folios. In several DIV1 units, opening words occur on the previous unit's final surface before the first inline XML folio milestone; overlapping boundary folios are therefore intentional.

object.description: The corresponding manuscript texts, with shared boundary surfaces assigned to both adjacent units

scope.notes: Manchester English MS 412 supplies selected passages where CME90174 changes its folio siglum to M.

relations.subjectLabel: University of Manchester complete digital viewer for English MS 412

reviewNotes: The primary subject is the checked-in repository XML, while the intellectual unit cataloged in the paired metadata manifest is an eighteen-text edited selection rather than a single manuscript work.
No single witness contains the complete CME90174 sequence. Manuscript, scholarly-edition, OTA-deposit, U-M-encoding, and repository-artifact layers are therefore kept distinct.

The current OTA ZIP is complete coverage of the provider's three-file deposit package, but that encoding is textually incomplete relative to Cigman's eighteen-part selection: it lacks actual sermon 13, preserves sermon 4 in corrupt form, and misnumbers the Sermon of Dead Men.
The General Corpus repository's software license is not treated as a license for the CME XML, EETS editorial matter, or repository-supplied manuscript images.

lineage.summary: The approved General Corpus source is the 2023 University of Michigan CME encoding CME90174. U-M obtained Oxford Text Archive item 0174, an incomplete and partly corrupt plain-text derivative or close associate of Gloria Cigman's 1989 EETS edition, then restored the missing sermon 13 and the damaged beginnings of sermon 4 from the print edition, reordered the material, and added structure plus folio references. Cigman's eighteen-text editorial selection transcribes sermons 1–11A from British Library Add MS 41321, sermons 12–16 and the Sermon of Dead Men from Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 751, and selected Sermon of Dead Men passages from Manchester English MS 412. The complete current OTA file package is cached. Manchester exposes a complete 199-canvas manuscript viewer, with the target work at fols 50r–72r / canvases 138–182, but no local bundle was made because the site's exact terms prohibit systematic extraction. No complete public facsimile was verified for the British Library or Bodleian witnesses.

catalogSummary.region: England; the language has been classified as South Central Midland or Central Midlands Standard, but the exact place of composition is unknown
catalogSummary.title: Lollard Sermons

catalogingSubject.label: Lollard Sermons
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the eighteen-text selection established in Gloria Cigman's EETS edition and represented by CME90174: sermons 1–16, an optional expansion designated 11A, and the Sermon of Dead Men. It is assembled from three manuscript witnesses and is not identical with any one codex. The cataloged work excludes the physical manuscripts, Cigman's 1989 edition and apparatus, the incomplete Oxford Text Archive deposit, the expanded 2023 U-M encoding, and the checked-in repository XML as distinct lineage layers.

contentParts.summary: A Middle English sermon or treatise on the four last things: death, judgment, hell, and heaven.

assertion.notes: OTA supplies this range without an argument. Pink regards the dating as uncertain and argues that composition in the last years of the fourteenth century is possible.
This is witness production, not a composition date; the current British Library record contains mutually inconsistent dating metadata for Add MS 41321.

dateStatements.display: First decades of the fifteenth century

extent.description: Eighteen top-level editorial/XML units: sixteen numbered sermons plus the separately encoded optional expansion 11A (seventeen sermon-cycle units in all), and the Sermon of Dead Men. Scholarship may instead count seventeen writings by treating 11A as an expansion rather than an independent work. Sermons 8–10 contain substantial alliterative-verse passages according to the British Library description, although the manuscript and XML present them as prose; verse-line counts are therefore not asserted.

formStatements.description: The collection is predominantly prose, but the official British Library description reports considerable passages of alliterative verse in sermons 8–10, copied and encoded as prose.

assertion.notes: The publisher synopsis describes the Sermon of Dead Men as a funeral sermon, while CME90174 encodes it as a treatise and the Bodleian catalog describes it as a treatise on the four last things; the genre label is therefore supported but not treated as an uncontested formal classification.

scope.description: Biblical lemmata, citations, and short formulas embedded in the Middle English sermons

notes: CME90174's HEADER places Cigman and the three manuscripts in AUTHOR elements; those mixed header fields are not accepted as medieval authorship statements.
The MED-stencil dates in the XML body are cataloging labels for witness-based text segments, not direct proof of composition dates.

The Add MS 41321 content-part ranges use cataloged incipit folios as well as XML milestones. Where two adjacent units share a manuscript surface, that boundary folio is intentionally present in both ranges; the first inline milestone can fall after a unit's opening words.

The record counts eighteen editorial/XML units. A scholarly count of seventeen writings is also compatible with the evidence when optional expansion 11A is treated as part of sermon 11 rather than as an independent work.
The word 'Lollard' is preserved for discovery and title fidelity while the qualified subject assertion and open question prevent it from functioning as an unexamined attribution.

next_steps: Consult Cigman's full introduction and the chronology arguments it cites.
Review post-2011 scholarship on the collection's dating and internal stratification.
openQuestions.question: What composition range can be defended for the individual sermons rather than inherited from OTA cataloging or inferred from witness dates?

next_steps: Consult Cigman's introduction, Patrick Hornbeck's 2006 article, and later sermon-by-sermon analyses.
Retain the title-tradition label separately from subject assertions.
openQuestions.question: How should the ideological relationship of each sermon to Wycliffism or Lollardy be classified?

next_steps: Inspect Cigman's textual and stylistic analysis and subsequent authorship scholarship.
Avoid assigning one named or collective author until the evidence distinguishes textual unity from editorial grouping.
openQuestions.question: Do the eighteen texts represent one anonymous author, multiple authors, or later compilation and adaptation?

assertion.notes: Kikusawa reports Smith's classification as South Central Midland and, more specifically, Central Midlands Standard. Manuscript scribal localization must not be treated automatically as authorial geography.

scope.description: Language represented across the edited selection

assertion.notes: No named medieval author is established. Differences among the texts have prompted suggestions of more than one writer, but this record does not convert that suggestion into separate author identities.

scope.description: Manuscript description in the edition's introduction
Language analysis in the edition's introduction

subjects.label: Preaching and the authority of the preached word

assertion.notes: The traditional title and some scholarship associate the texts with Lollardy, but their Wycliffite affiliation is contested and the sermons do not straightforwardly articulate every condemned Wycliffite position.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English collection of scripturally grounded sermons emphasizing preaching, moral instruction, and vernacular exegesis. Seventeen numbered gospel-sermon units follow lections for days from Advent through Lent, including an optional expansion to sermon 11 and an alternative to sermon 14; the separate Sermon of Dead Men treats the four last things. The received title associates the texts with Lollardy, but the strength and character of their Wycliffite affiliation remain disputed.

Eighteen encoded DIV1 units: sermons 1–16, optional expansion 11A, and the Sermon of Dead Men. Sermons 1–11A are based on British Library Add MS 41321; sermons 12–16 on Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 751; and the Sermon of Dead Men on Rawl. C. 751 with selected passages based on Manchester English MS 412.

The 18-text editorial unit is cataloged as an edited selection because no single manuscript contains the complete sequence represented by CME90174. Witness dates, edition dates, and encoding dates are kept separate from composition dating.

assertion.notes: This is the established editorial title. The adjective 'Lollard' preserves a scholarly naming tradition; it does not resolve the disputed degree of Wycliffite affiliation.

Seventeen numbered gospel-sermon units follow lections for days from Advent through Lent, including an optional expansion to sermon 11 and an alternative to sermon 14; the separate Sermon of Dead Men treats the four last things. The received title associates the texts with Lollardy, but the strength and character of their Wycliffite affiliation remain disputed.
